topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a closing door. A polite, sterile rejection. My stomach plummeted, a cold, heavy drop that settled deep in my chest. I reread the rejection letter three times, the words blurring into an unreadable mess. Then, I glanced over at Sarah, who was already animatedly discussing the upcoming research symposium with Professor Evans. Her laughter was bright, effortless, a sound that seemed to vibrate with undeniable success. I gripped my pen so tightly my knuckles turned white, focusing intently on the intricate pattern of the desk blotter, wishing, with a sudden, sharp pang, that the recognition I craved was simply mine.

[story 2]

I stared at the acceptance letter, the glossy paper mocking me from the desk. It was a stranger’s achievement, a smooth, golden ticket handed to someone else. My jaw tightened, a muscle ticking near my temple. I watched Mark across the crowded common area; he was leaning back, accepting congratulations with a relaxed smile that didn't require any frantic striving. I felt a strange heat rising in my cheeks, a prickly sensation that made me want to shrink into the upholstery. When I finally managed to push the paper away, the movement was too swift, too jerky. I started pacing the room, the rhythm uneven, a restless energy churning beneath my skin that felt entirely out of control.

[story 3]

The notification flashed on the screen, stark white against the dark background. A decision. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. I felt a sudden, intense pressure behind my eyes, a burning behind the lids. I looked towards the window, where the sun was streaming in, illuminating the dust motes dancing in the air—a perfect, unearned spectacle. I picked up my textbook, but the weight felt alien, cumbersome. I began to tap my fingers rapidly on the cover, a frantic, almost violent drumming that echoed the quickening beat of my own heart. I wanted to snatch the letter, to twist the words into something that sounded different, something that belonged to me.
